exemplar.title: The Ideal Dance Studio to Promote Creativity

detailedExemplarData.title: The Ideal Dance Studio to Promote Creativity

strengths: Effective labeling of ATL focus areas under the product goal, emphasizing the link between objectives and planned activities.
Thoughtful selection of research media-literacy and thinking-transfer ATL skills aligned with both the learning goal and project demands.
Series of mural prototypes that demonstrate iterative experimentation and application of ATL skills to improve design outcomes.
Well-constructed mind map that organizes the scope of media-literacy research, showcasing strong synthesis of ideas.
weaknesses: Interior design phases are outlined but not mapped to the project timeline for tighter integration.
Proof of media-literacy processes lacks direct examples of how specific sources influenced design decisions.
Research sources are listed without credibility evaluations, weakening media-literacy demonstrations.
Self-management narrative would benefit from concrete evidence (e.g., timestamps or screenshots) to substantiate consistent progress.
Weekly to-do table uses color-coding without a legend and fails to reference specific success criteria.
Feedback from the interior designer is described but not supported with direct quotes or revisions tied to those comments.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: **The student demonstrates strong awareness** of the ATL skills neededâresearch, thinking transfer, self-managementâand applies them through prototypes and visual planning tools. Yet **the application lacks depth of evidence**: source evaluation is absent, phases arenât sequenced in the timeline, and feedback loops arenât explicitly documented. Strengthening these areas with concrete examples, credibility analyses, and direct mappings between tasks and criteria will showcase more robust skill application.

strengths: Vivid, detailed description of the intended creation that clearly conveys the vision for two complementary model perspectives.
Strong connection to personal interest, linking lifelong dance experience with interior design to demonstrate motivation and contextual relevance.
weaknesses: Success criteria table is empty, lacking specific, measurable standards for evaluating the model.
Learning goal describes ATL skill development but omits measurable milestones to track progress.
Project planning table contains incomplete entries and inconsistent dates, with insufficient task descriptions, resources, and success-criteria references.
feedback.calculationMethod:
Mini report holistic evaluation: **The planning section excels** in articulating what the student intends to create and why it matters to them personally, leveraging their dance background to ground the project in authentic motivation.  
**However, it falls short** in operational detail: without populated success criteria and measurable learning targets, the student has no clear benchmarks to assess progress. The project timeline and task breakdown remain underdeveloped, making it difficult to ensure feasibility and maintain accountability over the work plan.

strengths: Reflection opening effectively frames the projectâs personal impact, creating a strong context for growth narratives.
Clear self-evaluation levels tied to success criteria, demonstrating reflective self-assessment.
Comprehensive visual (Figure 6) of the final model supports analysis of spatial functionality and realism.
weaknesses: Pie chart lacks accompanying qualitative comments, reducing depth of data interpretation.
Reflection notes skill growth but omits concrete examples of transferring those skills to new contexts.
Does not explicitly link reflections to ATL language, missing an opportunity to ground insights in the rubric.
Improved research skills are claimed without before-and-after evidence (e.g., sample notes).
Top band of the product evaluation rubric is incomplete and unsupported by specific evidence.
Creativity rating cites survey percentages but lacks participant quotes or qualitative justification.
Realism critique identifies scale inconsistencies without specifying which features deviate.
Genre-combination rating could be strengthened by detailing how design elements meet the needs of each dance style.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: **The reflection is well-framed** and demonstrates honest self-assessment against success criteria, reinforced by strong visuals. However, **it remains largely descriptive** rather than evidential: data visualizations need qualitative context, self-reported growth requires tangible before-and-after examples, and rubric evaluations must include specific evidence. By explicitly linking reflections to ATL skill language and inserting representative quotes or annotated comparisons, the student can transform general observations into rigorous, criterion-aligned evaluations.

annotations.comment: You outline interior design phases well but havenât linked them to your own timeline. Map each phase (programming, schematic, etc.) to your planning table for tighter integration.
Your proof of media literacy explains the process but should include direct examples showing how specific sources improved your understanding or influenced design decisions.
The success criteria table is empty. Populate each column with specific, measurable criteria aligned to your rubric levels so you can effectively evaluate your model against clear standards.
Your learning goal describes ATL skill development but lacks measurable targets. Define specific milestones (e.g., number of tutorials watched or mural drafts completed) to track progress clearly.
The project planning table has incomplete entries and inconsistent dates. Refine it with full timelines, clear task descriptions, resources, and success criteria references to ensure feasibility.
You list many research sources but donât evaluate their credibility. Apply source evaluation criteria (e.g., author expertise, publication date) to strengthen your media literacy approach.
The pie chart is clear; consider appending a sample of qualitative comments in the caption or alongside the chart to enrich your interpretation of the data.
Your self-management narrative is strong; enhance it by including screenshots or timestamps from your action plan to demonstrate consistent progress tracking.
The weekly to-do table uses color-coding without a legend and lacks links to success criteria. Add a key and explicitly reference which criterion each task addresses.
You describe feedback from an interior designer well. Strengthen this section by quoting specific suggestions and showing how each influenced your mural iterations.
Your personal reflection identifies growth but lacks concrete examples of applying these insights to other contexts. Add at least one instance where you transferred a skill to a new project.
You reflect on working independently but should explicitly link this to a named ATL skill (e.g., self-management) to meet the rubricâs language and criteria.
Your claim of improved research skills is strong. Show a before-and-after example (e.g., initial vs. final research notes) to illustrate this development concretely.
The intended creation description is vivid and detailed, effectively conveying your vision for two complementary model perspectives.
The product evaluation rubric is repeated but the top band row (7â8) is incomplete. Provide specific evidence from your survey or model to justify your rating in each category.
Your justification for a level 6 in creativity promotion cites survey percentages. Include representative participant quotes or brief notes to substantiate the emotional responses.
You note scale inconsistencies in your realism rating. Identify two specific features where scale deviated (e.g., door height, window width) to clarify your critique.
Your genre-combination rating is justified but could be strengthened by specifying how barre placement and storage capacity meet the needs of each dance style (e.g., cubic metres for tap shoes).
Clearly labeling ATL focus under the product goal highlights the connection between your objectives and planned activities.
Your reflection opening effectively frames the projectâs personal impact, setting a strong context for subsequent insights.
Clear articulation of personal interest linking your lifelong dance experience with interior design demonstrates strong motivation and contextual relevance.
Selecting both research media literacy and thinking transfer ATL skills shows thoughtful alignment with your learning goal and project demands.
Your series of mural prototypes demonstrates effective iterative experimentation and clear application of ATL skills to enhance design quality.
You assign self-evaluation levels and provide rationales, demonstrating clear self-assessment against your success criteria.
The mind map visually organizes your media literacy research scope effectively, showcasing strong planning and idea synthesis.
Figure 6 offers a comprehensive visual of your final modelâs layout, supporting your analysis of spatial functionality and realism.
